item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My headcanon? The Foundation-GOC alliance known as "AEGIS" used paratech to build a NOVA Bomb from the Halo series and yeet the entire planet into a cloud of dust. All while making a desperate attempt to get off-world through an extradimensional portal — and failing miserably in the process.

It is interesting to see that a prospective Foundation-GOC alliance would use anomalies to destroy the enemy, instead of just considering skips the enemy. It's a lot more awesome to see a sheer destructive force being used for the good of humanity rather than being wasted. That said, this article reads like two different articles stitched together, and the alien depiction errs heavily on the side of generic. But I do like "whatever it takes" stories where humanity fights tooth-and-nail for their own survival, so this gets an upvote — though a tenuous one — from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, this is one of my favorite SCPs, I’ve always loved series 3 and extraterrestrial skips. I feel like more detail and a slightly less redactions could make it more interesting and fleshed out, but over than that I love it. (Yeah I know this is an old skip, and I’ve read it quite a few times before, I just never made any comments)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
More reality bombs for less aliens.
A fine implementation of the best suggestion to any problem:"Get bigger guns"

Because some times, what I need is friendship between GOIs and a gigantic bomb.
Just sad that the Church of the broken God could not see it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1. I find it amusing that after all the changes, I still agree with the posters commenting on the original contest submission: yes, it's XCOM (not X-COM though, which is its failing) in SCP conditions… Except not.

I came here to make the same arguments as seen on the first two pages - the initial demolition of cities alone should have released dozens of skips which should have been reported on the news segment, and that's before the Foundation would start uncrating Euclids and the occasional Keter that could be used as a weapon or as a "your problem now, suckas" drop bear. Other 2000 entries showed that you CAN have effective cross-linking (or unlinked referencing) to flesh out a story, and that is what this entry sorely lacks. There's at least a dozen "we're convincing them it ain't World War III so that they don't indiscriminately kill us all" skips by now, several of which could have easily been told "there's the commies, sic'em!".

The apocalyptic log being done by a grunt that gives a layman's terms retelling is also kind of… Cliche?

In the end, it's a great tale plot fodder, but a bland and cliche story that is also not really fun as a skip (the debris we get from Australia is entirely mechanical and non-anomalous except for how it got here? Isn't that… Too easy?). So, here's my drop in a bucket of a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not upvoting for now. Although the writing is good, it just don't have a definite "skip-feel" to it to make it fit in with the SCP-verse.

I think it raises the question of what constitutes a "skip". It is hard to call it a skip. The alien is not paranormal. It feels more like just a very advanced race rather than something that is inexplicably supernatural. It can be explained - we understand the way they travel and their weaponry in layman scientific and military terms (eg. "drones"). More than that, we could actually put up a good fight against it…with conventional weaponry. As far as space-skip is concerned, I think its definition is leaned more toward planet-eating Cthulhu entities rather than Battle LA style alien. The fact that this skip is about just not the alien but also the space-time anomaly that brought the wreckage of the battle into our world is what make it pass for me, just barely, though.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The author is not currently active on the site. If you feel like you need their express permission, you can always send them a Wikidot PM.

item.title: Author
item.content:encoded: Do I have permission to link your SCP-2069 document in my SCP file?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish there was a way to upvote comments because that's exactly what I was thinking when I finished reading this too. Don't know what kind of threat would make the Foundation work with someone like the Chaos Initiative though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "use of weaponized SCPs and ultimately the detonation of a weapon of mass destruction utilizing anomalous technology did so much collateral damage to the fabric of reality that it started cracking in places" thing sounds like a GREAT idea. I would read an article about that.

I also think it would pair well with PFC Winters's comment about winning, but at a massive cost. What if the wreckage didn't cross realities when NOVA detonated, but some time later? I'm picturing a story where AEGIS beats the aliens, but Earth is still devastated, and they're still rebuilding a working civilization… and that's when they start finding the lasting damage. Energy fluctuations, time distortions, all kinds of freestanding anomalous effects, obviously centered around the places they fought and the weapons they set off.1 Then they discover they've got working access to other realities, and have to decide whether it would be ethical to use it to evacuate their broken world.

I imagine you could have a resolution where the wreckage eventually stops coming through, and the portal is quiet for weeks or months… and then a hard drive comes through, and then it closes entirely. The hard drive, on further inspection, contains the last deliberate communique from AEGIS: tactical data (what weapons they used, what they learned about the aliens, what worked, what didn't, what the side effects were, how to contain them if they open up again, and an account of how the Foundation successfully entered a cooperative venture with the GOC and other GoI), and a simple message. "Don't make our mistakes."

Footnotes
1. Maybe this is where the original investigation started on our end- our reality is one of the ones they partially punched through to, so where they have a spot that's receiving nine or ten times normal sunlight, we have a spot that's dark no matter how much light you shine on it, or something like that. The Foundation goes to investigate this small anomaly, and then starts noticing others in the same area, and eventually the wreckage and the messages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's kind of cliche, but I love it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not certain, but is it more than coincidence that AEGIS is a synonym for SHIELD? Because that's definitely the vibe I'm getting, and I'm honestly surprised that no one else mentioned it.

The SHIELD/X-COM/Foundation combination going on here could be interesting, but as others have mentioned, it's not quite delivering. As someone whose name escapes me at the moment, this would probably work better as a tale where the aliens come bearing super-alien-technology and AEGIS ends up fighting back with super-anomaly-technology, magic, the legions of hell, etc. It gets a tiny bit into that at the end, but again doesn't really deliver.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a bit of Tiberium Wars, except the Foundation and GOC are not nearly as antagonistic as GDI and NOD were to each other before the Scrin showed up. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alternate universe shenanigans? Or a SCP-2000-type rewrite? Either way I'm a sucker for all those sorts of things, and having lurked in the chatroom while author and youse guise talked about alternate timeline of this SCP, I want to see moar +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man yes, that is exactly the tale I wanna read. If I had half a brain I'd write it. Gonna have to work on that I guess, maybe a happy Christmas story about how everyone put their differences aside because they hate aliens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I envision some sort of Justice League-Secret Society style team-up where the various special skills of all the major groups in the Foundation-verse are brought to bare to both make that happen, and also ensure no one but us and them realize it. Foundation, GOC, MC&D, Chaos Insurgency, Serpent's Hand, and the more cool-headed of the CotBG, Horizon Initiative and Mana-Charitable.

What good are any of our problems with one another if we lack a planet to fight them over on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have been really slow on reading all the 2000s, but I really like this one. Mostly for the GOC and the Foundation rearing their heads out of the rubble and slapping the invaders as hard as possible.

Actually you know what I would love as an addition to this? Some kind of addendum on this where we find out that the reason the invasion didn't happen here was that because the Foundation/GOC/SuperConspiracyTeamUp used some combination of skips to move the planet in response to the planned relativistic barrage. Like they just blip time and rewind the Earth's revolution around the sun a bit. No further detailed explanation on what happened to the rest of the automated invaders or the joined forces. In my imagination, the idea that we had the ability to literally make our planet duck a bullet might have convinced the armada to just turn around and find someone else to bother for fear that the next planet we move is put on a collision course with them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do like how everything gets fucked over.
sadly it's pushed to some sort of alternate universe =(
+1 anyway

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm in the same boat as the other commentors. This is an average-to-good SCP that opens the door to what could easily be my favorite canon scenario. I'm a sucker for clever resource utilization (like trying to weaponize the SCPs still in containment. I also love a good invasion story when the "victorious" invaders discover they're in trouble. I'd love, for example, to see an AI's view of 632 getting lose.
Years ago, when X-files ended, and Mulder and Scully drove into the sunset to (IIRC, this might just be headcanon) recruit supernatural forces to fight the (magic-vulnerable) alien invasion, I wanted to see that plot. This essentially has that potential. The tricky thing would be successful characterization of alien robots, but this will get my upvote, if only because of the narrative door it is opening.

One quibble, however. You seem to have stumbled into accidental "quasi-plagiarism" with AEGIS. In the tabletop RPG "Conspiracy X1," the characters work for AEGIS, a secret organization dedicated to the containment of paranormal and extraterrestrial threats. You might want to change the designation of the new combined force.

Footnotes
1. An excellent RPG, BTW. I'd recommend it to SCP fans, as it can have a similar tone, and is just a generally good read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not voting either way for now but I like it.

Unlike some of the other posters, I don't really have anything against standard alien invasions, and I think that this is a good alien invasion, all things considered. The only thing that disappointed me and that I would contend, is that it didn't depart enough from the standard apocalyptic invader that's "so far above us primitive humans" which I like but, given the in-universe we're working in, I can't really see happening just so.

From an in-universe POV, at this point I can't really buy that a tag team of both the SCP Foundation and the GOC wouldn't give alien invaders -ANY kind of alien invader- a good run for their money, given the many, many paranormal, anomalous and downright reality-bending resources they have at their disposal. Not to mention the additional (passive, if you like) resistance that the plethora of paranormal objects running around on earth would give any alien too (I'd think that just destroying NYC would probably release a bunch of dangerous skips into the world these conquerors-to-be are trying to inherit and would have to deal with).

No rant version: I guess what I'm trying to say is that, while I think it's a very well-done "alien invasion" narrative, it lacks a more… decisive SCP-universe twist that would make it really stand out for me.
To be fair, I do think you tried to bring attention to the fact that humanity was indeed making use of whatever paranormal resources they had at their disposal to take the planet back, I just don't think you committed strongly enough to, and took enough advantage of that (really cool) twist. This may have just been me, but the article lead me to expect to see more developments in that vein, and the promise didn't deliver.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It grabbed my attention and I liked it, but I feel you could have used the whole 'using SCPs to fight aliens who are not used to SCPs' thing a bit more. Also maybe one or two more fragments from surviving civilians.

I still liked it enough to +1 but I hope you expand on this in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my personal favourite 2000 entry, and I love the controversy/mixed opinions it appears to have stirred. Very well executed, although I do agree that the final two addenda should be swapped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It didn't quite live up to the concepts full potential (as fully expressed by others up-thread) and I would like to have seen a bit more justification of the continuum-hopping wreckage. But I enjoyed the read. That is all that matters in the end.

I found the similarity of the item designations and the date format slightly confusing at times. too many "2000-##" in a row. using a YYYY/MM/DD date format would make that list slightly easier to read.

A random thought that just occurred to me, why no EMPs? That would be the obvious weapon to try once the resistance figured out they were fighting drones. But would an EMP even leave forensic evidence on wreckage? I have no clue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well… As you know, I had the same reaction as most people did to the article. On re-read I feel identically. Great promise, great writing, but it doesn't pay off enough for me.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Similarities to Independence Day aside, it is a good story, I'm just not sure it's a good skip. The bits of junk that washed up on shore may as well be anomalous items, or neutralized at the very least. They don't do anything, they represent something, and we know what that something is thanks to one of the bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What still seems to be a mystery is the time shift, although I believe it might be hinted on in the Nova operation very faintly. In that case, I'd however imagine it'll be something along the lines of some kind of a global explosion ( as per the term "Supernova") than a time shift. The thought of SCP F and GOC joining forces to fight off a common enemy is very intriguing and captivating in a romantic sense. Using SCPs for warfare as the last resort is also something I approve of in a tale or an article.

Another thing that I have been seeing here for quite a while is the expository letter containing everything trope repeated in several 2000 proposals, including for example the Marianas trench document. I am not saying it is used here badly, but it's slowly becoming something seen quite often.

An interesting thing to think about is whether the Foundation currently containing the 2000 artifact had actually experienced the conflict, but it is not elaborating further on the incident, possibly dissolving their alliance with GOC and separating ways.

All in all, I see this as Terminator, except from space, and I see this entry as very basic and average for this contest and I will not upvote, nor downvote. The story grabbed me, and I feel it lacking in the amount of grabbing content the other successful proposals I have upvoted have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding what Choiwel said: your average alien invasion, yet it was well-written enough to keep my attention from beginning to end.

That said, I kind of agree with the folks upthread. It was set-up to be amazing, and didn't end quite as amazing as it should have. I'd like to have seen this as the canon you were planning Aelanna. Because just from this, it would be pretty dang awesome, I think. Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a cliche alien invasion, yet you managed to catch my rapt attention while I read it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it before and I like it now. It's all very, very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think a consensus is developing on this article that we are being set up for so much, and then kind of left hanging. This is a premise that I agree with. The front part of the article sets us up nicely with the alien invasion and the alternate reality, and then…it's an alien invasion and an alternate reality. Both of these things can be written as well as one likes (and in this case they were indeed well written per your usual standards), but neither concept is enough on its own to pull off something as ambitious as what everyone is expecting SCP-2000 to be.

I am refraining from voting in the expectation that you'll come back to this one. I don't wish to force anyone to write what I want them to write, but I'm really hoping that the nascent "the Foundation fights back with its array of horrible mind-breaking atrocities" gets the development that it deserves. I mean, that would be a wonderful twist on the technologically advanced aliens attacking the hapless humans. "The puny humans stand no chance against us with their primitive projectile weaponry, and- Skarlaxx, why is there a teddy bear made out of ears in the command room?"

So much potential that you've got here. I hope to see something come of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There has to be some personal narrative.

This is fair, I've added some more personality to PFC Winters, does that help?

Which is, in essence, nothing but an alien invasion.

Yes, that's correct. I was hoping to portray a catastrophic event that had nothing to do with anomalies, a sort of take-that at the various GOIs showing that their petty squabbles meant nothing in the long run because they got beaten up by legitimate, non-anomalous invaders while focusing so much on their infighting. This was supposed to be balanced out by the hopeful note that the SCPF and GOC do manage to set aside their differences and join up as a dream team to lead the counterattack.

I would like this article a lot better if the aliens just stayed in orbit and bombed us into oblivion. I would like it if it did something with an alien invasion, because you can't have cool writing, you can't have cool characters or character development, and plot's irrelevant in alien invasion stories because come on, an alien invasion is just an excuse plot at this point. Maybe explore one of the common objections to alien invasions? Why the fuck are they coming for us? What'd we do to them? Where do they come from? Stuff like that.

Yes, you are entirely right. This was originally intended to be a canon with a lot more exploration of the events leading up to NOVA, and a lot of people have mentioned that it shows. I know it's not an acceptable excuse, but I did intend to have a tie-in tale that presented the aliens' side of things; I even toyed with shoehorning it into the main article at some point, but it just didn't fit with the main narrative.

[…] i don't think lasers…cut. certainly not cleanly.

Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[…] more fleshed-out […]

That is really unfortunate wording…

[…] which… blows shit up and sends it back into the past/to an alternate universe.

The intended implication was that the use of weaponized SCPs and ultimately the detonation of a weapon of mass destruction utilizing anomalous technology did so much collateral damage to the fabric of reality that it started cracking in places. I've refined a few points to make this somewhat more clear, does that help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't feel like it lives up to it's premise. The idea of hard sci-fi aliens coming up against the Foundation/GoC has promise, but the execution just sorta peters out. With that premise, I wanted to see a progression in the news reports/logs, starting with the the hard horror of the alien bombardment and transitioning into madness as the Foundation opens the gates and lets every bit of hell they have at their disposal loose on the invaders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the last two addendums should be swapped. The ending was a lot less moving than the letter before it. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm wavering between a no-vote and a downvote.

Something like this is super-dependent on execution, and shunting it off to an alternate universe just seems…cheap. Cheap enough to harm the entirety of the article. It could have worked as a scip, but it'd need a load of finagling with the format. I'll have to ponder this further.

e: No-vote. It's not bad, but it is very disappointing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was originally going to neutral vote, but after reading the discussion page and seeing the points made here, plus reading other, interesting takes on aliens, I have to downvote. I really wish you had used this in a canon, because the twisting you had to do in order to make this work as an SCP seems to have crushed everything which may have once made it interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am a neutral vote on the brink of downvote.

So first of all I've been growing steadily more iffy over the past couple days about alt-foundations. We just have so fucking many of them. It's absurd. But that's not a huge problem. If the alt-Foundation's got a twist or the article has a hook, we're golden. This brings us to my main problem with this article.

It's well-written, sure! But it's an alien invasion. It's Independence Day; it's Battle: LA; probably a bunch of novels as well; probably more films that I didn't list; probably a few more video games. Here's the thing. An alien invasion in itself is just really boring at this point. It's as boring as zombies. There has to be some personal narrative. I'll read a novel about an alien invasion if I can get some cool writing or nice character insight. But this is an SCP article, it can't have either of those. So problems arise. This article is nothing but an alien invasion.

Well, okay. This article, if I'm reading it correctly, is the wreckage of an alien invasion generated in the aftermath of some superweapon explosion which was so powerful it punched through space and time and dumped all this shit on our planet. Which is, in essence, nothing but an alien invasion. Even the news reporter sounds like a news reporter from a movie about an alien invasion. Even the letter.

I would like this article a lot better if the aliens just stayed in orbit and bombed us into oblivion. I would like it if it did something with an alien invasion, because you can't have cool writing, you can't have cool characters or character development, and plot's irrelevant in alien invasion stories because come on, an alien invasion is just an excuse plot at this point. Maybe explore one of the common objections to alien invasions? Why the fuck are they coming for us? What'd we do to them? Where do they come from? Stuff like that.

man that brink is beckoning me closer

edit: oh, really minor quibble—you seem to be going for hard-ish scifi, what with the relativistic kill vehicles and whatnot, and the thing is, i don't think lasers…cut. certainly not cleanly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm just not feeling it. I think an article about a no-shit alien invasion of the planet Earth should have more interesting or more fleshed-out invaders than this — I mean, we know all of jack shit about the invaders, except that they use robutts. And we fight back with… regular weapons. And the only cool thing here that really puts this in the Foundation universe as opposed to the "Generic Alien Invasion Defier V (Defy Harder) for PC and PS4" is the use of an anomalous weapon, which… blows shit up and sends it back into the past/to an alternate universe. As opposed to something, well, cool.

This is like five kinds of squandered potential, and it shows. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Sorry to be a doc-block, though, but if the name of this really is AEGIS, there's already a skip named Aegides. Not that it's an uncommon word.

In any case, have a plus one for a great take on the classic invasion movie plot.

E: On second thought, neutral vote.
